perturbation.original_text: Permitted Recipients means those persons who have a need to know the Confidential Inform ation for the Permitted Purpose and who are officers, directors, employees, agents or contractors (at any level) of the Receiving Party or its affiliates, or anyone else for whom the Receiving Party takes responsibility for under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Permitted Recipients means only the direct employees of the Receiving Party who are directly involved in the Project and have signed an individual confidentiality agreement, excluding any agents, contractors, or affiliates, regardless of their level or confidentiality agreements they may have with the Receiving Party.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition of 'Permitted Recipients' is broad, including officers, directors, employees, agents, contractors, and affiliates. The modified definition restricts it to only direct employees and requires an individual confidentiality agreement, conflicting with section 3.4 (b) that states '(b) are bound in writing by confidentiality terms, policies or procedures no less restrictive than those in this Agreement.' The modified definition creates an In-Text Contradiction due to narrowing definition of Permitted Recipients and requirements of an agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.4 Before disclosing any Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party to the Permitted Recipients, the Receiving Party shall identify to the Permitted Recipients that it is the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information and is subject to disclosure and usage restrictions, and shall ensure that the Permitted Recipients have: (a) execut ed Appendix A – Permitted Recipient’s Acknowledgement; or Page 3 of 6 (b) are bound in writing by confidentiality terms, policies or procedures no less restrictive than those in this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The changed text significantly narrows the definition of 'Permitted Recipients' from a broad group including agents, contractors, and affiliates to only direct employees, requiring individual confidentiality agreements. This directly contradicts section 3.4, which allows for Permitted Recipients to be bound by confidentiality terms, policies, or procedures that are 'no less restrictive' than the agreement itself, implying that not all Permitted Recipients need individual agreements. This change creates a direct conflict and incompatibility between the definition and the requirements outlined in section 3.4, leading to an in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 3.1 Except as expressly permitted by this Agreement, the Receiving Party shall not disclose the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information and shall use reasonable care, which is at least the same degree of care that the Receiving Party ordinarily uses with respect to its own proprietary information, in relation to the use, transmittal and storage of the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.1 Except as expressly permitted by this Agreement, the Receiving Party shall not disclose the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information. However, the Receiving Party is only required to exercise the same degree of care that it uses in protecting non-critical information, unless the Disclosing Party provides a written notice specifying a higher degree of care required.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause mandates 'reasonable care' at least equal to the care used for the Receiving Party's proprietary information. The modified clause introduces a lower standard (care for 'non-critical information') unless specific notice is given. This change contradicts section 6.1 and 6.2, weakening the protection expected. The contradiction lies in the uncertainty of standard of care now required. The original text, there was clear liability with reasonable care and damage.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 6.2 The Receiving Party shall indemnify the Disclosing Party against any loss or damage suffered by the Disclosing Party as a result of the failure of the Receiving Party or such other persons who are disclosed Co nfidential Information under this Agreement to comply with the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The changed text lowers the standard of care required of the Receiving Party regarding Confidential Information from 'reasonable care, which is at least the same degree of care that the Receiving Party ordinarily uses with respect to its own proprietary information' to 'the same degree of care that it uses in protecting non-critical information, unless the Disclosing Party provides a written notice specifying a higher degree of care required.' This contradicts section 6.2, which holds the Receiving Party liable for any loss or damage resulting from failure to comply with the terms of the agreement. The original text provided a clear standard for liability, while the changed text introduces ambiguity about the standard of care and liability.

perturbation.original_text: 5.3 Subject to section 5.4, within 10 days of a written request by the Disclosing Party, the Receiving Party shall, unless otherwise precluded by any legal obligation: (a) return to the Disclosing Party or destroy all of the Disclosing Party’s original tangible Confidential Information, together with all ta ngible copies and reproductions; (b) subject to subsection 5.3(c) , make its best efforts to delete all electronic copies of the Confidential Information received from the Disclosing Party; (c) safeguard any electronic Confidential Information that cannot be destroyed due to the Receiving Party’s archiving practices or policies with the same degree of care as it would its own Confidential Information; and Page 4 of 6 (d) retain electronic Confidential Information described in subsection 5.3(c) only for the period it normally maintains such records, which electronic Confidential Information shall remain subject to the provisions of this Agreement until destroyed.
perturbation.changed_text: 5.3 Subject to section 5.4, upon the earliest of (i) a written request by the Disclosing Party, (ii) the termination of the Project, or (iii) the expiration of three (3) years from the Effective Date, the Receiving Party may, at its sole discretion and unless otherwise precluded by any legal obligation, either (a) return to the Disclosing Party or destroy such portions of the Disclosing Party’s original tangible Confidential Information as the Receiving Party deems reasonable, together with such tangible copies and reproductions as the Receiving Party is reasonably able to locate; (b) make a good faith effort, but without any obligation, to delete all electronic copies of the Confidential Information; and (c) retain electronic Confidential Information described in subsection 5.3(c) for as long as the Receiving Party considers necessary, which electronic Confidential Information shall only be subject to the provisions of this Agreement to the extent that the Receiving Party affirmatively chooses to apply such provisions.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause mandates return or destruction of Confidential Information within 10 days of a written request. The modified clause introduces significant discretion for the Receiving Party regarding timing ('earliest of...', 'may') and scope ('such portions as the Receiving Party deems reasonable'). This contradicts section 5.2, which specifies a continuing obligation of confidentiality for 2 years after the Initial Term. The uncertainty about data retention, and which data is retained under confidentially creates the in-text contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 5.2 Any disclosure of any Confidential Information to the Receiving Party made during the Initial Term shall continue to be subject to this Agreement for a period of 2 years thereafter, provided that any trade secret shall be entitled to protection for the life of the trade secret.
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates return or destruction of Confidential Information within 10 days of a written request, creating a clear obligation. The changed text, however, introduces significant discretion for the Receiving Party regarding timing ('earliest of...', 'may') and scope ('such portions as the Receiving Party deems reasonable'). This directly contradicts section 5.2, which specifies a continuing obligation of confidentiality for 2 years after the Initial Term. The modified clause's uncertainty about data retention and the discretionary application of confidentiality directly clashes with the continuing obligation detailed in section 5.2, creating an in-text contradiction, because it creates ambiguity to whether data will be subject to continued confidentially agreements.

perturbation.original_text: 9.8 This Agreement shall be governed by and interpreted under the laws of the Province of Alberta and the Parties consent to the exclusive jurisdiction of the courts of the Province of Alberta for all disputes arising out of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 9.8 This Agreement shall be governed by and interpreted under the laws of the Province of Alberta; however, the Parties agree that any disputes arising out of or relating to this Agreement may be submitted to binding arbitration in accordance with the rules of the Alberta Arbitration and Mediation Society, and the decision of the arbitrator shall be final and binding on both Parties.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause specifies exclusive jurisdiction in the courts of Alberta. The modified clause allows for disputes to be submitted to binding arbitration, potentially outside the court system. This introduces a contradiction regarding the dispute resolution mechanism. This clause is in-text contradicting with clauses 6.1 and 6.2, now requiring to seek remedies by arbitration.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 6.1 Each Party acknowledges that monetary damages would be inadequate to protect the other Party against any actual or threatened breach of this Agreement, and, without prejudice to any other rights and remedies otherwise available to the other Party, agrees to the granting of injunctive relief in favour of th e other Party without proof of actual damages.
perturbation.justification: The original clause 9.8 mandates exclusive jurisdiction in Alberta courts. The changed clause introduces the option of binding arbitration, which can occur outside the court system. This directly contradicts the original exclusive jurisdiction. Clause 6.1 states that either party can seek injunctive relief in court without proof of damages. Requiring arbitration as a first step undermines or negates the ability to seek immediate injunctive relief from the courts, creating a contradiction.
